beats.headline: A wiper erases a global shipping line overnight.
beats.body: Self-spreading malware destroyed tens of thousands of machines at a major shipping company. It came back only because one backup happened to be offline and out of reach during the attack.
say: Every gap, every shortcut, every forgotten door — I will find it. And if I can't get in, I'll burn it down.
Not tonight. Seal the roads, guard the vaults, and prepare for the dark.
Prepare for the worst, you mean.

beats.title: THE VAULTS
say: If I can't break the walls, I'll snatch your secrets in transit, or wait for the lights to go out.
Then we seal the roads, guard the vaults, and prepare for the dark.

beats.title: THE VAULTS & LIFELINES
beats.subtitle: Communications · Media · Contingency
say: Layer six protects the data itself, and keeps the city alive through disaster.
Three families hold the line: System & Communications Protection, Media Protection, and Contingency Planning.

beats.title: THE VAULTS & LIFELINES
say: SC, MP, and CP. The warden of the roads, the steward of scrolls, and the keeper of lifelines.

beats.persona: The Warden of Walls and Roads
beats.protects: Fortifies the ramparts and seals the roads between districts.
beats.reality: Boundary protection and cryptography that protect data in transit and at rest.
say: The Warden of Walls and Roads, System and Communications Protection.
It fortifies the ramparts and seals the roads between districts.
In reality, that's boundary protection and cryptography — guarding data as it moves and where it rests.

beats.plain: The line you draw around a system: everything inside is 'your system' — the part you own, authorize, and defend. Drawing it well is one of the most consequential decisions in 800-53.
beats.example: Like the property line and fence around a building — it defines exactly what you're responsible for guarding.
say: What is 'the edge of the system'?
Draw a line around your computers, data, and the people who run them. Inside is your system. SC-7 watches that line.

beats.title: Boundary Protection
beats.plain: Monitor and control traffic at the system's boundaries.
beats.why: The boundary is where outside meets inside; SC-7 decides what may cross.
say: SC-7, boundary protection, guards the edge of the system.

beats.plain: Math that scrambles data with a secret key, so anyone who intercepts it sees gibberish. 'In transit' is data moving across a network; 'at rest' is data sitting on a disk or in a database.
beats.example: The padlock on a web address (HTTPS) is encryption in transit; a password-locked, encrypted laptop is encryption at rest.
say: You keep saying cryptography — what does it actually do?
It locks the data. Encrypt it in transit and at rest, and a thief without your key gets nothing.

beats.title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
beats.plain: Protect data in transit — its confidentiality and integrity.
beats.why: On the wire, encryption is what stops eavesdropping and tampering.
say: I was listening on that line...
Then you heard noise. SC-8 protects data in transit, often with approved cryptography.

beats.title: Protection of Information at Rest
beats.plain: Protect data at rest — in storage.
beats.why: Stored data outlives the session; SC-28 keeps it encrypted and safe.
say: And the data just sitting in storage?
SC-28 protects it at rest, often with cryptography from SC-12 and SC-13.

beats.title: The boundary decides what crosses
say: Untrusted traffic meets the boundary, and only the allowed passes through.
Filter at the edge, encrypt across the wire.

beats.quote: Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system;
say: Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system;

beats.title: System and Communications Protection
bullets: SC-7 Boundary Protection — control traffic at the boundary
SC-8 Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity — protect data in transit (often encrypted)
SC-13 Cryptographic Protection — use approved cryptography
SC-28 Protection of Information at Rest — encrypt data at rest

beats.oath: Filter at the edge; cipher on the wire.
say: The Warden's oath. Filter at the edge; cipher on the wire.

beats.protects: Guards every scroll, and destroys the ones discarded.
beats.reality: Protecting digital and physical media, and sanitizing it before disposal or reuse.
say: The Steward of Scrolls, Media Protection.
It guards every scroll, and destroys the ones discarded.
By 'media,' NIST means storage: hard drives, SSDs, tapes, USB sticks, even printed paper. MP protects them — and wipes them before they're thrown away.

beats.title: Media Sanitization
beats.plain: Sanitize media before disposal or reuse — truly erase it.
beats.why: A discarded drive can still spill its data; MP-6 makes erasure final.
say: MP-6, media sanitization, ensures deleted really means gone.
Your old backup drive would have told me everything.

beats.title: Media Use
beats.plain: Restrict the use of removable media like USB drives.
beats.why: Removable media carries malware in and data out; MP-7 controls it.
say: And MP-7 controls removable media, the humble USB stick.

beats.plain: NIST writes controls as templates. Text in brackets like [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] is a blank YOU fill in — every 30 days, each quarter, whatever your risk calls for. The catalog gives the requirement; you set the value.
beats.kicker: HOW TO READ A CONTROL · PARAMETERS
say: One reading tip before the next gold card. Notice the brackets in the catalog's text.
So the brackets are blanks the organization fills in?
Exactly. The requirement is fixed; the value is yours.

beats.quote: Sanitize [organization-defined system media] prior to disposal, release out of organizational control, or release for reuse using [organization-defined sanitization techniques and procedures] ;
say: Sanitize [organization-defined system media] prior to disposal, release out of organizational control, or release for reuse using [organization-defined sanitization techniques and procedures] ;

beats.title: Media Protection
bullets: MP-2 Media Access — restrict who can access media
MP-4 Media Storage — store media securely
MP-6 Media Sanitization — sanitize media before disposal
MP-7 Media Use — control removable media
say: Data outlives the device. Wipe it like you mean it.

beats.oath: When it's gone, it's gone for good.
say: The Steward's oath. When it's gone, it's gone for good.

beats.protects: Plans for the day the towers fall, and how to rebuild.
beats.reality: Backups, alternate sites, and tested plans to recover operations after a disruption.
say: The Keeper of Lifelines, Contingency Planning.
It plans for the day the towers fall, and how to rebuild.
In reality, that's backups, alternate sites, and tested plans to recover operations after a disruption.

beats.title: System Backup
beats.plain: Back up your systems and data — and be able to restore.
beats.why: When ransomware strikes, a clean, tested backup is the way home.
say: CP-9, system backup, a clean copy you can actually restore.
'Clean' is the key word — a backup kept offline or unchangeable, where ransomware can't reach it. That's what saved the shipping line we opened with.

beats.title: Contingency Plan
beats.plain: Write and maintain a contingency plan — and CP-4 tests it.
beats.why: An untested plan is a guess; rehearsal turns disaster into routine.
say: CP-2 is the plan; CP-4 is the drill that proves it works.

beats.quote: Conduct backups of user-level information contained in [system components] [frequency];
say: Conduct backups of user-level information contained in [system components] [frequency];

beats.title: Contingency Planning
bullets: CP-2 Contingency Plan — have a contingency plan
CP-4 Contingency Plan Testing — test the plan
CP-9 System Backup — back up systems and data
CP-10 System Recovery and Reconstitution — recover and reconstitute
say: Survival is planned in advance, never improvised.

beats.oath: We rehearse the dark, so we survive it.
say: The Keeper's oath. We rehearse the dark, so we survive it.

beats.title: The Vaults & Lifelines in the RMF
bullets: SC and MP carry out the PROTECT function for data
CP delivers RECOVER — resilience when prevention fails
In practice: encrypt in transit and at rest, wipe media, test backups
say: Prevention buys time. Recovery buys survival.

beats.q: Ransomware encrypts your servers. What gets you back fastest?
say: Scenario: ransomware hits. What brings you home? Pause, and answer.

beats.q: A surplus hard drive is sold without being wiped. Which control failed?
say: And the drive that left with its secrets intact?

beats.title: Episode 06 — the Vaults & Lifelines
lines: SC draws the boundary and encrypts data in transit and at rest (SC-7, SC-8, SC-28).
MP protects storage media and truly wipes it before disposal (MP-2, MP-6, MP-7).
CP backs up (clean = offline/immutable) and rehearses recovery (CP-2, CP-4, CP-9).
beats.mnemonic: Prevention buys time; recovery buys survival.
say: Notebook: lock the data moving and still — and keep a backup the attacker can't touch.
Data sealed, lifelines ready. The city can take a punch.
Then I'll bring everything, all at once. Siege night.
We'll be ready. All twenty guardians, together.
